title: AI Concepts: How It Works
subtitle: Teaching Machines to Think!
overview: This session demystifies Artificial Intelligence — how machines learn from data, recognise patterns, and make decisions. Students explore the journey from raw data to trained models, understand neural networks through human brain analogies, and discover how AI powers the tools they use every day.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — neural network diagrams, training data examples, confusion matrix, decision tree, loss curve graph.

keyTopics: AI vs Machine Learning vs Deep Learning — Nested concepts: AI is the umbrella, ML learns from data, DL uses neural networks
How ML Works — Data → Features → Algorithm → Training → Model → Prediction
Supervised Learning — Learning from labelled examples (spam/not spam, cat/dog)
Unsupervised Learning — Finding hidden patterns in unlabelled data (clustering, grouping)
Neural Networks — Layers of connected nodes inspired by the human brain — input, hidden, output
Training & Loss — Model adjusts itself to reduce prediction errors over many epochs
Overfitting vs Underfitting — Memorising training data vs failing to learn patterns
Real-World AI Applications — Image recognition, voice assistants, recommendation engines, self-driving cars

activity.title: Train Your Own AI — Image Classifier with Google Teachable Machine
parts: Laptop or tablet with browser and webcam
3 categories of objects to classify (e.g. pen, eraser, ruler)
Whiteboard to draw the AI pipeline diagram
steps: Open teachablemachine.withgoogle.com and click 'Get Started' → 'Image Project' → 'Standard Image Model'
Create Class 1 — hold up a PEN in front of the webcam and record 30+ samples
Create Class 2 — hold up an ERASER and record 30+ samples
Create Class 3 — hold up a RULER and record 30+ samples
Click 'Train Model' — watch the training progress bar and loss graph update in real time
Test the model in the Preview panel — hold up each object and observe confidence percentages
Try testing with objects the model has never seen — discuss: why does confidence drop?
Draw on whiteboard: Training Data → Feature Extraction → Model → Prediction → Confidence %
Challenge: Add a 4th class for 'nothing' (empty hand) and retrain — observe how the model improves!

quiz.q: What is the correct relationship between AI, Machine Learning, and Deep Learning?
options: They are three completely separate fields with no overlap
Deep Learning is the broadest field, containing Machine Learning and AI inside it
AI is the broadest field — Machine Learning is a subset of AI, and Deep Learning is a subset of Machine Learning
Machine Learning and Deep Learning are the same thing
quiz.explanation: AI ⊃ Machine Learning ⊃ Deep Learning! AI is the big umbrella — any technique making machines smart. ML is AI that learns from data. Deep Learning is ML using multi-layered neural networks.

options: Programming a computer with exact rules for every situation
A type of robot that learns by walking around
A method where computers learn patterns from data and improve their performance without being explicitly programmed for every case
Teaching machines to repair themselves
quiz.explanation: ML = learning from data! Instead of writing rules manually, you feed examples to an algorithm. It finds patterns on its own and uses them to make predictions on new, unseen data.

options: A teacher watching students learn in a classroom
Training a model on labelled data where correct answers are provided — the model learns to map inputs to outputs
An AI that learns by exploring without any guidance
Training that only uses images as input
quiz.explanation: Supervised Learning = labelled examples! You show the model 1000 photos labelled 'cat' or 'dog'. It learns the difference. Then it can classify NEW photos it has never seen. Supervised = teacher provides correct answers!

options: Training without a GPU
Learning from labelled data where answers are pre-set
Finding hidden patterns or groupings in unlabelled data without being told the correct answers
An AI model that runs without supervision from engineers
quiz.explanation: Unsupervised Learning = no labels! The AI explores the data and finds natural groupings on its own. Example: clustering customers by shopping behaviour — nobody told it the groups, it discovered them!

quiz.q: What is a NEURAL NETWORK inspired by?
options: The structure of a computer's CPU
The way the internet routes data through servers
The biological structure of the human brain — billions of interconnected neurons passing signals
The layout of a printed circuit board
quiz.explanation: Neural Networks = brain-inspired! Just like your brain has billions of neurons connected by synapses, a neural network has nodes connected by weighted links — signals flow through layers to produce an output.

quiz.q: In a neural network, what are the THREE types of layers?
options: Start, Middle, End
Input, Hidden, Output
Data, Process, Result
Learn, Think, Decide
quiz.explanation: Input Layer = receives raw data (pixels, numbers). Hidden Layers = extract features and patterns. Output Layer = produces the final prediction (cat/dog, spam/not spam). Deep Learning = many hidden layers!

quiz.q: What is TRAINING in machine learning?
options: Installing the software needed to run AI programs
The process of feeding data to a model so it adjusts its internal parameters to minimise prediction errors
Writing the code for an AI application
Testing the AI on new data it has never seen before
quiz.explanation: Training = the AI going to school! You feed it thousands of examples. It makes predictions, checks if they are wrong (loss), and adjusts its weights to do better next time — repeated over many epochs!

quiz.q: What is LOSS in machine learning?
options: The amount of data lost during training
The number of neurons removed from a network
A number that measures how wrong the model's predictions are — lower loss means a better model
The time taken to train a model
quiz.explanation: Loss = the AI's score of wrongness! If the model predicted 'dog' but the answer was 'cat', the loss is high. Training keeps adjusting until loss is as low as possible. Watch the loss curve go DOWN during training!

options: When a model is trained on too little data
When a model memorises training data so perfectly that it performs poorly on new, unseen data
When a model trains for too few rounds
When a neural network has too few layers
quiz.explanation: Overfitting = too much memorising, too little learning! The model learns the training examples by heart — including noise and errors — but fails on new data. Like a student who memorises answers without understanding!

options: When a model is too complex for the data
When the training dataset is too large
When a model is too simple to capture the patterns in the data — performs poorly on both training and new data
When the model trains for too many epochs
quiz.explanation: Underfitting = the model did not learn enough! It is too simple — like trying to draw a curved boundary with only a straight line. It performs badly on training data AND new data. Need more complexity or more training!

quiz.q: What does a CONFIDENCE PERCENTAGE mean in an AI prediction?
options: How fast the AI made the prediction
How many training examples were used
How certain the model is about its prediction — higher percentage means stronger confidence
The accuracy of the training dataset
quiz.explanation: Confidence % = how sure the AI is! '92% cat' means it is very sure. '51% cat' means it is barely guessing. In Teachable Machine, you see these percentages live — low confidence shows the model needs more training data!

quiz.q: Which of these is a real-world application of AI image recognition?
options: Sending an email
Saving a file to a USB drive
Unlocking your phone with your face using Face ID
Printing a document
quiz.explanation: Face ID = AI image recognition! Your phone's neural network was trained on thousands of faces to recognise YOURS specifically. It analyses 30,000+ infrared dots of your face to unlock in milliseconds!

quiz.q: What is a DATASET in machine learning?
options: The code used to build a neural network
A collection of examples (data + labels) used to train and evaluate a machine learning model
The output produced by a trained AI model
The set of rules programmed into an AI manually
quiz.explanation: Dataset = the AI's textbook! It is a collection of examples the model learns from. More diverse, high-quality data = smarter model. Garbage in = garbage out — data quality is everything in ML!

quiz.q: What is a DECISION TREE in machine learning?
options: A diagram showing how a neural network is structured
A flowchart-like model that makes decisions by asking a series of yes/no questions based on data features
A tree planted near a data center for cooling
A type of training algorithm for deep learning
quiz.explanation: Decision Tree = AI flowchart! It splits data by asking questions — 'Is it bigger than 50cm? Yes → Is it green? No → Probably a flamingo!' Easy to understand and visualise — great for explaining AI decisions!

quiz.q: What does Google's Quick Draw demonstrate about AI?
options: How AI controls robotic arms to draw pictures
How a neural network can recognise hand-drawn sketches in real time by learning from millions of human drawings
How AI generates images from text descriptions
How computers store image files efficiently
quiz.explanation: Quick Draw = AI trained on 50 million drawings! As you sketch, the neural network recognises patterns in real time and guesses what you are drawing. It gets smarter with every new drawing added to its dataset!
